locked_facts.current_conservation: Electric current is the same at every point in a series circuit — I_before = I_through = I_after resistor
locked_facts.resistor_role: A resistor opposes the flow of charge but does not consume or reduce current
locked_facts.voltage_drop: A resistor causes a voltage drop across itself: V_R = I × R
locked_facts.series_total_resistance: Total resistance in series: R_total = R1 + R2 + R3
locked_facts.series_current_formula: Current in series circuit: I = V_total / R_total
locked_facts.charge_conservation: Charge is conserved — electrons are not created or destroyed in a resistor

ncert_truth_statements: Electric current is conserved in a series circuit — the same current flows through every element
A resistor causes a potential difference across it but does not reduce the current flowing through the circuit
For resistors in series, the total resistance equals the sum of individual resistances: R = R1 + R2 + ...
The current through each resistor in a series combination is the same
Ohm's law applies to the whole series circuit: I = V_total / R_total
common_misconceptions: Current gets used up by a resistor — FALSE
Less current flows after a resistor than before it — FALSE
A bigger resistor consumes more current — FALSE
